data.title: Sezzle: Buy If You Want Your Money To Fizzle
data.description:
Introduction

Sezzle Inc. (NASDAQ:SEZL) is a fintech company that operates in the Buy Now, Pay Later space. They offer payment solutions in-store and online at a variety of retail brands, and they provide proprietary solutions that connect consumers with instant lines of credit at the point of sale. Most of their loans are Pay in 4 installments, but they also have other cadences available to customers. The company also offers a virtual card and a variety of other products in its portfolio that cater to a variety of customers. Since their inception, they have transacted over $10B in gross merchandise volume and have over 16M sign ups in users.

SEZL has ballooned over the past 2 years in share price. Year to date, the shares are up ~145%. Since May 2024, the stock is up over 860%. This is outstanding performance, driven by strong user growth and beneficial partnerships, but at this point, with shares at all-time highs, we believe that it is time to short the company.

It is now time to sell SEZL for 2 main reasons. Firstly, the current macro environment lends itself to a poor time for BNPL names. Secondly, there are increasing concerns with valuation that should be highlighted.

Market Dynamics are shifting against SEZL

In their May 2025 investor presentation, SEZL notes that a core part of their target market are younger credit consumers. However, and they explicitly say this in the materials, younger consumers struggle with below-average credit scores.

The issue with going after this customer base is simple. Despite being the segment that could most use and benefit from BNPL and breaking up purchases into installments, there is a reason their credit score is lower - they have not proven to be reliable borrowers in the past. Whether that is an indication or not of how trustworthy they will be in the future on their loans and credit is to be determined, but credit score is a massive financial indicator in this country. According to SEZL, 78% of their consumers are between the ages of 18-47, and 71% are using BNPL products to better manage their cash flows. Sure - this is a great product idea in the sense that it does allow more consumers to purchase products that they may not have the budget for in the first place, but the issue remains that just because someone can afford the first payment or two, does not mean this group of consumers will continue to make payments / afford the ongoing payments.

Furthermore, SEZL notes that nearly half (48%) of their customers are individuals who have been rejected for a credit product, at least once, in the past year. This raises a red flag for us - if big banks and credit unions, with their myriad of creditworthiness analytical products, say no to a customer, why is SEZL taking on the leftovers / rejects? While credit score is not the end all be all to how financially strong a customer may be, it definitely paints an accurate depiction. Remember the last time everyone started going after subprime customers? That did not end too well.

This hypothesis appears to be playing out in reality. Klarna, a private competitor, just announced that their net losses have doubled in the first quarter even though their revenue and user count grew. A major reason is that across the industry, consumers are starting to default and miss payments on their balances. LendingTree states that 41% of users in the past month have paid a loan late, up from 34% in the previous year. This is alarming. Additionally, over 25% of users have started taking out loans to pay for groceries, an absolute necessity. This is equally alarming. As the job market continues to waver, M&A activity remains stagnant, interest rates stay high, and other factors that impact the financial flexibility of this target segment (e.g., continuation of student loan repayment) we expect the average consumer to be tightened even more financially, which means more users for BNPL but likely more losses and late payments as well. When the next revenue stream for BNPL names is fast food purchases, one must wonder if things are getting too out of hand. For SEZL, after a year of incredible share appreciation, these market factors are holding up a large sign saying pause.

Competitors

As prudent investors, before we make any investment decisions on any given equity, we look at its competitors. We see competitors as potential alternatives that have similar businesses and macro impacts but possibly at a cheaper cost. For this analysis, we look at PayPal (PYPL), Block (XYZ) and Affirm (AFRM).

Let’s start with the most common approach: P/E ratio.

AFRM does not show a P/E as it is not profitable yet. However, you can see that SEZL's P/E is very high compared to the rest, at around 2x of PYPL and XYZ.

Now let’s look at P/S.

SEZL once again stands out from the pack, but not in a good way. It has roughly 2x the P/S ratio of the next highest competitor (AFRM).

Now, a higher valuation on a couple of metrics does not always mean that a company is overvalued. There could be other factors at play such as book value, cash flows or growth. In the case of SEZL, a fast-growing company, we are particularly interested in seeing if revenue growth can justify the lofty valuation.

This chart shows how the revenues of each company have grown since 2021. SEZL has not always had the highest growth but in the last FY 2024 especially, they stood out from the pack. Adding up the total grown from FY 2021 through FY 2024 we see:

This time, SEZL also stands out but now in a good way. They have the highest growth out of the pack at 136% with AFRM not too far behind at 106%. Does this mean that SEZLs high valuation is justified? We think maybe it is when compared to PYPL or XYZ. However, we are not so sure about AFRM.

AFRM has roughly 4.5x the revenue of SEZL at $1.5 billion in the TTM, compared to SEZLs $329 million. Additionally, while AFRM is not profitable like SEZL, they are approaching profitability with only a net loss of $62.2 million in the TTM compared to a loss of $517.8 million and $985.3 million in the last 2 FYs. Finally, AFRM has a large cash position of $1.4 billion that can help fuel their continual growth and market reach and a healthy current ratio of 13.47. SEZL on the other hand only has $88.9 million in cash and a current ratio of 2.62.

Overall, it is clear that in terms of valuation metrics, SEZL is quite overvalued compared to peers. Some of this over valuation could be explained by SEZL’s high growth, but it does not fully justify it, especially when compared to AFRM.

Risks to the thesis

There are 2 risks to the thesis that should be highlighted.

Valuation Improves: SEZL has shown high growth as we have seen, and they have recently achieved positive net income and are not showing signs of slowing down. One risk to our thesis is that SEZL continues to show high growth, beating out expectations significantly. Overall, we do not see this as a huge risk. The company in its Q1 earnings call revised 2025 revenue growth to 60-65% which in the best case (65%) would mean that 2025 revenues will be $447.3 million compared to the 2024 revenue of $271.1 million. The reported P/S of 11.3 was based on the company’s TTM revenue, which was $329.1 million. So doing some rough math to calculate the P/S with $447.3 million revenue:

(11.3 * 329.1 million) / 447.3 million = 8.31At a P/S of 8.31, SEZL will still be overvalued compared to AFRM's 5.45 P/S by ~1.5x. Our conclusion: even SEZL’s best-case revenue scenario will result in a relatively higher valuation compared to competitors.

M&A Activity from a Larger Financial Player: If SEZL becomes part of M&A talks or activity, that would quickly boost the stock price further, leading to the thesis being wrong. There have been no active or recent mentions of any sort of activity like this being discussed at the moment, but it is always something that a smaller (by market cap) fintech like this could be engaged with. Players who could be looking to acquire SEZL include large banks, competing BNPL firms looking to merge, and possibly, if the company can continue to show steady financials, private equity firms focused on the consumer tech sector. However, with M&A activity still not really picking up yet, this remains a lower risk.

Conclusion

To summarize, SEZL has grown astronomically over the past year or so, and it’s time that investors cool off on buying this stock. The current macro dynamics of US consumers feeling unstable, paired with the fact that SEZL targets a less than prime segment of the consumer market, means that they would be more exposed to consumers pulling back on spending / missing payments. As mentioned above, with firms like Klarna, signs of consumers missing payments and leading to losses are already starting to show.

In addition to the macro risks, SEZL is also demonstrably overvalued compared to its peers, so an investor interested in investing in buy now, pay later likely has better options.

For now, SEZL is a sell.

data.title: IEEPA Tariffs On Hold Following Court Ruling
data.description:
The US Court of International Trade (CIT) has rejected the Trump administration’s use of the International Economic Emergency Powers Act to apply the “Worldwide,” “Retaliatory” (CIT definition) and “Trafficking” tariff plans. The CIT ruled the Executive Orders enacting the tariffs “are declared to be invalid” as they “exceed the authority of the president to regulate importation by means of tariffs.”

The order, which takes the form of a summary ruling, gave the administration 10 calendar days to put its order into action. The Department of Justice appealed the ruling to the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, which in turn has ordered the tariffs remain in place while the appellate process continues.

Depending on the outcome of the Court of Appeals review, the administration may also appeal to the Supreme Court for a final ruling in an attempt to quicken the judicial process. A delay in the repeal of the tariffs could be as long as several months as the judicial process unfolds.

Normally, federal cases can take years to reach the Supreme Court, with the high court often sending cases back to lower courts for further clarifications. However, due to the importance of the CIT decision, the appeal process could be resolved in a matter of weeks or months.

If the appeal court stays the CIT decision, leaving the current tariffs in place until it reaches a decision, a delay in the repeal of the tariffs could be as long as several months as the judicial process unfolds.

If the IEEPA tariffs are ultimately removed, the administration is likely to look for alternative mechanisms to apply the tariffs. These could include reframing the IEEPA tariffs to meet the CIT’s concerns or wider use of sector-specific investigations using the Section 232 (national security) or Section 201 (safeguarding) review processes, among other options.

The CIT ruling does not include the Section 232 reviews of seven industries that are underway, nor the three already in place, which together account for 42% of US imports and were in any event excluded from the IEEPA tariffs.

Actions under the Section 232 review results could be brought forward to allow the administration to demonstrate continued action on tariffs, with the president recently flagging that tariffs on smartphones could be increased to 25% as soon as late June.

Should duties be removed, this would reduce the pressure on ongoing negotiations with countries to sign improved trade conditions. Part of the CIT’s ruling was based on the administration’s argument that IEEPA was being used to “pressure” countries into reaching a deal.

Continuing negotiations

At a minimum, the ruling makes the July 9 deadline for most countries and Aug. 12 for mainland China irrelevant, though negotiations may continue because of the chance that a Supreme Court appeal succeeds or that the CIT decision gets narrowed. That could prove particularly important for situations where negotiations are proving contentious or where elections are complicating the negotiating process.

Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum is likely to continue tariff negotiations with the US despite the court suspension, particularly as she seeks to bring forward the review of the USMCA from 2026 to the second half of 2025. Separately, negotiations on the 25% duty on steel, aluminum and automotive imports are likely to continue, as these were not affected by this court process.

Countries with higher-than-average exposures to the existing and ongoing Section 232 reviews, including South Korea and Japan in the case of steel, aluminum, autos and electronics, and for the EU in regard to autos and pharmaceuticals, also have an incentive to continue negotiations.

Even with IEEPA relief, a significant portion of mainland Chinese exports would remain exposed to other tariff mechanisms (such as Section 232 or Section 301), should the Trump administration pursue them. Sectors where mainland China dominates global supply chains (including consumer electronics, telecommunications equipment or solar panels) would be particularly vulnerable to Section 232 national security reviews.

With regard to the overall impact on supply chains, the US administration still has other channels to increase import costs, so businesses may still not have enough certainty to plan their investments and production. The sectors that would benefit most from the IEEPA duties being overturned are consumer goods industries where mainland China has a high proportion of US imports.

Among the larger import sectors those include toys (75.8% imported from mainland China in 2024), homewares, and audiovisual equipment, among others. All three are also highly seasonal and face sourcing uncertainties heading into the peak shipping season, which typically involves exports from Asia running from June through September.

Thus far, companies have focused on tactical actions including inventory front-loading, price increases and cost negotiations, and may continue to do so during the period of tariff uncertainty.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: MEG Energy: Let Us Talk About Strathcona's Offer (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have never been a fan of MEG Energy (OTCPK:MEGEF). However, that does not mean that the company needs to immediately give in and head to the offer made by Strathcona Resources (OTCPK:STHRF). The major problem with this is that Strathcona itself has not been a public company all that long as currently constituted. Strathcona took over Pipestone to go public back in 2023. Since then, management has been rearranging the portfolio of the now public company. This is evidently one of those steps. But I disagree with the announcement to sell some very promising assets to buy this company.

The reason is that MEG is a low-cost operator of a product that is discounted to the light oil benchmark because it is thermal oil. There is always the risk that during a downturn, that discount will expand to make even lower cost operations unprofitable enough to have negative cash flow.

The startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion Project pipeline has given a false sense of security to some as this has temporarily strengthened prices to keep the discount at lower levels. However, as is the case with many commodities, when the profits increase (as they have in this area of the industry) announcements to increase production proliferate. That likely means that Strathcona is very likely to "buy high" which is not what you want to do in this business.

Now, could the idea work out? Of course, it could. Anything is possible. But then again, one of the buyers of the properties sold is ARC Resources (OTCPK:AETUF). That management has long been known to be one of the more profitable in the industry. This company sold condensate producing property that a company like ARC definitely wants because condensate sells for a premium to light oil.

The claim is that Strathcona will now proceed to make as much or more money with a discounted product as it would have made with a premium product that one of the better managements in the industry purchased. To me, that requires a whole lot of explaining (and with lots of detail too) as the logic is a bit of a stretch to say the least.

ARC Resources had a goal to dramatically increase free cash flow before this "gift" came along and Strathcona made that objective much easier. In fact, the objective could be raised as a result of the transaction.

Then there is the natural gas production, just before both Canada and the United States are about to see a significant increase in the ability to export natural gas. Furthermore, even without that ability, things like Data Centers and other natural gas uses were promising a natural gas pricing recovery.

The more I look at this, the more the deal looks like "buy high and sell low". If I were a MEG shareholder, I would want no part of a management that is doing something like this. I have covered MEG Energy for some time.

The last article noted that the production of MEG Energy may well become a political target. That whole situation has not yet settled. It could, in fact, take some time to calm down and be replaced with a permanent agreement. But in the meantime, it looks far more likely that the United States will head into a recession from all the uncertainty. MEG Energy would likely do much better in such a situation as an independent.

On the other hand, the recent announcement of an Alberta wildfire threat is a seasonal situation that the industry in Canada has long adjusted to. Usually, the threatening part of this is not all that significant to the industry, and it is over with about as fast as it gets started. But the key here is "usually" has there have been some rare but costly situations in the past.

May 30 Response

The MEG board is moving at a rather deliberate pace to respond to the offer. Generally, MEG has in the past fought to remain independent. I suspect that will be the outcome of the current situation.

The premium offered for MEG shares is honestly not all that significant, although it is at the higher end of industry offers in the current atmosphere of these types of transactions.

It is really about can Strathcona manage MEG better than MEG can. I personally view it as "jumping from the frying pan into the fire" for shareholders because I do not see from the current Strathcona actions where that management will be superior to the current management.

First Quarter

The heavy oil and thermal oil parts of the industry have reported improved pricing. This has led to all sorts of robust results for this part of the industry.

Clearly, MEG is no exception to the industry trend. The long-term issue is that while it is clearly in each company's interest to increase production during this time of robust results, it is not in the interest of the overall industry to go back to the situation that existed before the pipeline expansion projected began.

Nonetheless, it does look like history will repeat itself (even if it takes a couple of years).

MEG is probably much better off expanding vertically to acquire some refining capability. That would make far more sense for this type of producer, as it avoids the whole discount situation while heading towards value added products that refineries produce.

Summary

MEG Energy was offered a small premium to its price at the time of the offer. Personally, I am not at all sure that waiting for that premium is worth the effort. On the other hand, MEG Energy as an independent is a relatively low-cost operator that can handle whatever the industry future deals to the company.

The Strathcona preliminary moves to purchase MEG Energy make me wary of any ideas that Strathcona can do better with the MEG assets than MEG can.

For those with a short-term view of things (and are disciplined enough to walk away with whatever profit they make) MEG Energy could be a buy. For many, it is a hold as they watch from the sidelines to see if this management can improve from its previous record now that it has a relatively new CEO. That is where I sit.

While MEG could have done much better for shareholders in the past, that is now "water over the dam". MEG has a chance to do better for shareholders now, and there are a lot of potential ways to achieve that.

Risks

The thermal oil that is the chief product here is imported by the United States, while the United States makes money exporting premium light oil. But that whole arrangement is now "under fire" in the political arena because tariffs on imports have been threatened.

My own guess is that this profitable situation will remain after a lot of posturing and bargaining. But there is no assurance that will be the outcome.

The pipeline expansion project has increased demand through the ability to export. The issue here is all the announcements I am fielding by covering various companies about expansion projects. That will restore the historical situation with thermal oil within the next few years.

That restoration would necessitate yet another pipeline expansion project. The first one took "forever". In fact, I covered the time when Kinder Morgan (KMI) sold the project to the Canadian government (likely in frustration).

From 2015-2020, the heavy oil and thermal industry was not profitable and at times had negative cash flows. A period like that can happen again for this discounted product.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3 Areas Of Focus As U.S. Earnings Strength Continues
data.description:
By Carrie King

The Q1 2025 earnings season showed us that U.S. companies started the year in a position of strength. Earnings for the S&P 500 grew more than 12% year-over-year, and sales expanded by 4.4%. Earnings outgrowing sales means that a strong period of U.S. profit margin expansion continues – a point I highlighted after Q4 2024 results came in. See the chart below. First quarter earnings were recorded before the tariff uncertainty shook markets in April. Yet, we know that, overall, companies should be able to absorb the shock of potentially higher prices and slowing demand.

A picture of profit prowess

Equity profit margins across regions, 2005-2025

A focus on earnings also helps investors avoid the danger of trading on volatile newsflow. The recent market recovery highlights the importance of staying invested and shows the opportunities that can be seized upon by fundamental equity investors when valuations drop.

The Q1 season also revealed the level of nervousness around equities amid geopolitical uncertainty. The share prices of companies that missed expectations were hit harder than they have been historically, while optimistic guidance was rewarded. This shows the value of in-depth company knowledge during times of volatility.

Here are three areas that caught our eye during the recent earnings season, all of which, we believe, are fertile areas of active opportunity:

1. Big tech defies the doubts

Earnings for the “Magnificent 7” grew around twice as much as those of the S&P 500, as big tech shook off any doubts that began to emerge after the Chinese “DeepSeek” artificial intelligence (AI) model demonstrated impressive performance in January.

We believe AI is a powerful long-term investment theme that will evolve and present opportunities for skilled stock pickers. Big-tech capital expenditure on AI investment showed no signs of slowing down in Q1. Some of the hyperscalers, such as Meta and Amazon, announced that capex was accelerating, while Microsoft and Google reiterated forecasts. We estimate the total for all hyperscalers for 2025 will be more than $370 billion, up from $230 billion in 2024, while operating cash flows stand at $739 billion. Crucially, hyperscalers are beginning to see returns on investment. Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg said, “AI is transforming everything we do.”

This investment is likely to flow through all areas of the AI technology stack, from data centers and power generation up to software and applications. Our tech team expects software companies to benefit as the technology becomes more readily available.

The DeepSeek news and recent tariff tumult show that many companies in this space were priced for perfection. Yet, recent pullbacks have presented opportunities for long-term investors to lean into the AI theme at an attractive price.

2. How about healthcare?

Healthcare earnings jumped by 44% versus Q1 2024, as positive developments were seen across subsectors from pharma to insurance. Yet, uncertainty surrounds the sector due to government announcements on drug prices and volatile tariff policies.

Most of the uncertainty concerns large-cap pharmaceutical companies, an area we have been underweight due to the persistent risk of patent cliffs. We prefer to lean into companies that specialize in medical devices and supplies, which benefit both from the stable earnings growth driven by ageing populations, as well as growth spurred by innovation. Some of the innovations here include AI-powered surgical robots, minimally invasive platforms and smart wearables.

Our healthcare analysts also spend a lot of time researching how trade uncertainty may hit healthcare earnings. Drug distribution companies, such as Cencora and McKesson, may be less impacted by tariffs than the broader sector.

And many of these opportunities are available at an attractive price, in our view. Healthcare remains cheap, at a 30% discount versus the S&P 500 – whereas historically, healthcare stocks have traded at a premium to the market.

3. A focus on financials

The best performing sector globally in 2025 has been the financial sector, driven by the continued boost from higher interest rates. U.S. financials delivered solid, if unspectacular, results in Q1 2025, growing earnings by just more than 6%. Despite these slightly lacklustre numbers, we believe momentum in the sector can continue, and our global financials team remains overweight the U.S. versus other regions. Economic activity and interest rates are in the sweet spot for bank profitability; increased investment is driving loan growth and capital market activity; and operating costs in the industry are decreasing on the back of AI adoption.

Bottom line

I wrote one quarter ago that U.S. “exceptionalism” was still alive and well. The subsequent months of market volatility caused many to question this label. The latest earnings season once again demonstrated the persistent strength of U.S. companies, and we believe there are opportunities across sectors to find quality companies capable of thriving even in periods of great uncertainty.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: MPC Container Transitions To New Era Of Accretive Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

MPC Container Ships (OTCPK:MPZZF) recently announced its Q1 results, along with a change in its capital allocation policy. MPZZF has long been a darling for divided-minded investors, posting double-digit yields for years. It aims to reduce dividends (while still returning capital to shareholders) to focus on opportunities it thinks will arise in the market off the back of new regulations. The stated goal of this approach is accretive growth and actively investing in retrofits in its fleet, thereby creating long-term value.

In a squeezed market for feeder to intermediate tonnage, this approach makes sense. MPC's low leverage, relatively new fleet, and great management team make it well-positioned for further growth in the intra-regional markets, which are forecast to grow more than inter-regional trade—still a buy.

MPC Container Ships in Brief

Headquartered in Oslo, Norway, MPC Container Ships trades on the Oslo Stock Exchange. CEO Constantin Baack has headed it since its founding in 2017. Its primary shareholder is MPC Capital AG, which holds about 35 percent of the shares.

MPZZF's business model involves providing "tactical" tonnage (i.e., smaller to medium-sized container ships) for the large liner companies. The liners mainly focus on the largest ships and often charter smaller ships to complete their networks.

It owns and operates 59 vessels, with an average capacity of 2,400 TEU, making it a large player in the feeder space. Its contract backlog is $1.1 billion (about 2 years of revenue), with 96% and 77% of available days fixed for 2025 and 2026, respectively.

MPZZF has no debt maturities until late 2027, when a $24.4 million balloon payment is due. For context, it had $219 million cash on hand at the end of Q1. Its gross debt at the end of the quarter was $507 million, and its net debt position (liabilities less cash on hand) was $288 million – 21 percent of total capital employed.

New Capital Allocation Strategy: Long-Term Growth Instead of (Massive) Dividends

The slide below sums up MPZZF's new capital allocation strategy and its rationale. Let's unpack this slide from its Q1 presentation:

It will no longer aim for dividend payouts of 75% of net profits (The Wayback Machine has archived the previous dividend policy) but rather 30-50%. The basic argument is that the company perceives permanently higher volatility, in part caused by geopolitical shocks (such as the Red Sea) and in part by the regulatory environment, where U.S. trade policy is the primary driver.

The recently announced IMO Net Zero Framework, although its implications are not fully clear, also plays a part in MPZZF's decision to change its capital allocation strategy. The enticing aspect of this framework is the global pricing mechanism for emissions. The framework calls for set thresholds of GHG fuel intensity. Ships emitting above the set threshold will have to "balance" their emissions by using "remedial units." Those units are acquired by contributions to the "IMO Net Zero Fund." Ships emitting below thresholds will be "rewarded" by the fund. In other words, it is a mechanism to transfer wealth from high-emitting ships to low-emitting ships.

From now on, MPZZF will (in the words of CEO Constantin Baack in the company's Q1 earnings call):

(..) focus on accretive deals for P&L and long-term value and we will seek active investments into the existing fleet including retrofits significantly advancing the vessel's performance, and tail end value of the respective vessels.

MPZZF aims to have the necessary flexibility (retain more capital) to make opportunistic deals in the market.

The market responded (to its Q1 statement, which included the news of the new capital allocation strategy) by sending the share down 12 percent. This harsh judgment reflects an overreaction, along with divided-minded investors realizing their positions in the fact of this strategic pivot. The company has been known for its double-digit yields. In the five trading days since the announcement, it has added about 8 percent.

Market Outlook

Intra-Regional Trade Set to Grow

I have been harping on this idea for a long time. The world has been somewhat divesting from China for years, and the current U.S. trade policy exacerbates this development. As there is no "other China" ready to take up the slack, manufacturing will continue to spread to several countries, thereby creating new trade lanes and strengthening others - ultimately increasing demand for MPZZF's core assets, the small to medium-sized container vessels (<8,000 TEU).

As shown above, MPZZF expects these segments to perform well (citing MSI and Clarksons as sources).

Total Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio 30%, But Only 6% for MPZZF's Core Segment

The second part of the slide linked above shows the following split of the somewhat large global order book:

The important thing to remember is that the small- to intermediate-sized ships operate in different trade lanes than the larger ships. For the smallest ships, the business case for retrofits might not add up. If intra-regional trade (which is served by the small-to-intermediate segments) does indeed grow, there is little pressure from new ships coming on water.

Major Risks and Factors to Consider

A return to navigating the Red Sea for commercial shipping would have a profound impact on markets. In its Q1 earnings call, MPZZF's CEO Constantin Baack estimated the effect of the diversion around the Cape to be a 12% increase in TEU miles. This effect would be undone if ships were able to use the Suez Canal again, and likely result in significantly lower freight rates.

At least implicitly, MPZZF puts weight on the IMO Net Zero Framework's expected ability to drive changes in the shipping markets. The framework will not come into full effect until 2027, and it remains to be seen precisely what the consequences will be. MPZZF will need to balance its investments in emerging technologies so as not to "over-invest" in a future market mechanism that is not fully finalized.

As MPZZF trades in NOK, the smallest of the G10 currencies, investors should consider the effect of this pairing in their portfolios. Additionally, investors should check if they are eligible for lower withholding taxes on dividends.

Conclusion

This article has reviewed MPC Container Ships' changed capital allocation strategy, which focuses on accretive growth instead of the highest possible dividend yield. It also signals a wariness in management, as challenging regulatory conditions and geopolitical instability create an unpredictable environment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Energy Recovery's Tariff Impact Is Minimal Post U.S.-China Agreement
data.description:
Energy Recovery (NASDAQ:ERII) released 1Q25 results earlier this month, with operations within guidelines, but with a very negative comment about the China business ($9 million), and the removal of guidance on Wastewater.

However, this single negative comment was rendered obsolete five days after the earnings release, when the US and China reduced the reciprocal tariffs they had set. The lost China business comment was relevant at 150% tariffs, whereas post-agreement tariffs were 10%.

Still, the post-earnings fall in price, almost 50% at some point, and 18% below the price at which I issued my first Buy rating, has not fully recovered. Considering the company's expectations for the year, and long-term advances in CO2, I believe the name continues to be a speculative Buy.

1Q25 results

Desalination down on bumpiness: The desalination business is driven by large megaprojects, which have lumpy revenues, and more recurrent OEM and after-market demand. The megaproject side of the business did not generate revenues in Q1 because of contract and shipment timing. This led to a 30% fall in revenues in the segment ($8 million from $12 million a year ago). Still, management believes FY25 guidance for the segment is within reach. We should remember that the same happened last year in 1Q24 and 3Q24.

In the shareholder letter for the quarter, the company recognizes that it is a little behind schedule, with 30% of the guidance already contracted, vs 60% in 1Q24 and 50% in 1Q23. However, it believes the high-probability pipeline (to be closed in Q2) should reach 80% of guidance.

CO2 slow advancement: The company had promised in Q4 that one refrigeration OEM would be piloting the PIX product in the FY25 summer. The number has now increased to three OEMs. One of these OEMs' names was released, Hillphoenix, supposedly the largest CO2 refrigeration OEM in the US (I was not able to independently verify this).

Reduction in OPEX: The company had guided for OpEx of $70/73 million this year, but the figure has been revised to $69/72 million. The run rate could be even lower with 1Q25 at $17 million ($64 million annualized), including almost $900 thousand in non-recurring expenses.

Tariffs' impact is already obsolete

The company provided a relatively complete analysis of the impact of tariffs on costs and revenues. We have to remember that ERII manufactures most of its products in the US, and then exports them.

The impacts were originally heavy on costs from components imported from China that are re-exported ($5/6 million in additional costs), and also from products exported to China ($9 million in FY25 projects at risk, or 50% of the wastewater segment guidance for the year).

However, only days after the earnings release, the US and China negotiated a stall in tariffs, and a reduction from the trade-halting tariffs of 250% and 150%, to 30% and 10%, respectively.

This implies the impact on costs should be around $2 million, and that the impact on projects should be close to $0 (as the 10% incremental cost can be absorbed or passed to customers, whereas a 150% incremental cost simply destroys the whole business).

The company removed the guidance for wastewater, but I would believe that it will reinstate it in 2Q25, barring any changes in US-China tariffs.

Revisiting the valuation

ERII now trades at a price about 20% lower than when I first considered it a speculative Buy in February 2025. The company's prospects have not changed much.

Back then, I considered the name could generate about $50 to 55 million from the desalination and wastewater business, and that it would invest about $16 million in the CO2 business (SG&A and R&D). This investment could be lost, but it was not a cost of the water business.

Therefore, I considered that the name could generate about $30 million in net profits while investing in new ventures. The company has used excess cash in the past to buy back shares, so that these earnings become an actual yield of about 4.5% on the current $675 million market cap.

In addition to this, if the company abandoned the CO2 business, it would still own the water business, and would generate up to $18 million more in earnings (from the after-tax profits today invested in CO2). That would elevate the yield to 7%. That yield is not particularly attractive to be honest, but is the 'worst case scenario' where the company's CO2 projects do not work, desalination does not grow, and wastewater is also abandoned.

If CO2 works, in addition to wastewater and desalination, the company projects (investor presentation) that it can grow at 15% CAGR for the next four or five years. That revenue CAGR can translate to much higher earnings CAGR because the company's gross margins are very high (65% in the desalination business) and OpEx is relatively stable.

Assuming that CO2 economics are similar to water, we could be talking of a combined operating margin of 35%, or about $90 million in operating income by the end of the decade.

I believe the perspective of a 5/7% yield on a worst-case scenario, plus the optionality to build something much bigger within this decade, is an attractive proposition. For that reason, I maintain a Buy rating on ERII.

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies which have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. data.title: LVMH: Scarcity Sells In An Age Of Abundance
data.description:
Introduction

LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) the world’s largest luxury-goods conglomerate, and it keeps proving its resilience in an unsettled global economy. The group’s portfolio of prestigious “maisons” (from fashion flagships Louis Vuitton and Dior to jeweler Tiffany & Co.) thrives by balancing supply with demand. LVMH practices controlled scarcity, limiting distribution, rolling out small, tightly edited capsule collections and using waitlists to heighten anticipation for must-have pieces. The result is a level of exclusivity that lets the company set prices on its terms across every division. In short, LVMH’s entire investment story hangs on that scarcity playbook: by making less, the group earns more, posting sturdy margins and steady expansion even when the broader economy stumbles.

Controlled Scarcity Safeguards Brand Equity

At Louis Vuitton and Christian Dior, LVMH’s flagship fashion maisons, products are intentionally kept scarce and distribution is tightly controlled. These brands never chase volume at the expense of exclusivity; they limit sales largely to their own boutiques, avoid broad discounting, and often release limited-edition capsule collections that sell out quickly. Louis Vuitton’s revival of its Takashi Murakami collaboration after twenty years shows the strategy in action: the bags were produced in small numbers and sold out on pent-up demand. Dior likewise runs waitlists for new handbags rather than flooding stores. Because demand outruns supply, both labels maintain the aura of rarity that supports premium pricing and healthy profit margins. Crucially, this approach eliminates the need for markdowns or outlet sales, preserving full-price integrity and reinforcing each brand’s luxury image.

Scarcity Proves Its Worth in Tough Times

Scarcity gives LVMH a cushion when the economy wobbles. Because products are hard to find, shoppers who can afford them usually keep buying at full price instead of waiting for discounts. When demand cools, the company doesn’t slash prices. It simply makes fewer items, keeping margins healthy and the brands’ image pristine. The first quarter of 2025 proved the value of that approach: organic sales slipped a modest three percent while many mass-market retailers fell much harder. Target’s comparable sales dropped 3.8%, Kohl’s net sales fell 4.1%, and fast-fashion giant H&M warned of a weak start to spring after only a 1% uptick in March sales. Investors prize that kind of consistency, rewarding LVMH with a valuation premium few rivals can match.

Engineered Scarcity Across Segments

Fashion & Leather Goods: This largest business group exemplifies LVMH’s volume-versus-value philosophy. In Q1 2025 Fashion & Leather Goods revenue was €10.1 billion, down 5 percent organically from the exceptional post-pandemic surge a year earlier, yet that dip still counted as “good resilience,” achieved with no broad promotions. Louis Vuitton and Dior kept demand strong by rolling out fresh collections. Dior’s newest handbags launched with solid sales even though production was intentionally limited.

Watches & Jewelry: Short runs and familiar flagship lines kept shoppers engaged, and revenue stayed roughly level year over year despite a tough 2024 comparison. Bulgari and Chaumet focused on tightly controlled batches of high-end pieces that carry healthy margins.

In timepieces, TAG Heuer and Hublot stirred collectors’ interest with special editions, while Tiffany & Co., the newest name in the portfolio, expanded its signature lines into more cities worldwide.

Selective Retailing: This segment (Sephora, DFS, department stores) held up well, with organic revenue off only 1 percent. Sephora grew on a high base by sticking to a premium assortment and standout in-store service. Le Bon Marché posted “a good start to the year,” helped by exclusive events and curated products.

Financials

Q1 2025 revenue landed at €20.3 billion, a modest 3 percent organic decline from Q1 2024’s exceptional showing. Wines & Spirits dropped the most at 9 percent organic, while Fashion & Leather Goods slid 5 percent. Perfumes & Cosmetics and Selective Retailing were roughly flat, and Watches & Jewelry was stable. This mix of soft spots and stable areas shows the business still holding its ground against last year’s high bar. LVMH did not report quarterly profit, but its margins have historically sat near record highs, and full-price selling likely kept them that way. Strong brands and tight cost control usually turn revenue into cash quickly because inventory moves at healthy margins. Management says it is “both vigilant and confident” for the rest of 2025, pointing to innovation, brand spending, and selective distribution as the main growth drivers. Management’s tone implies a high-single-digit operating-income increase for 2025, predicated on a second-half stabilization in China and a low-single-digit rebound in the United States.

Risk

Even a powerhouse like LVMH faces hazards. A sharp downturn in global luxury demand could hit spending on high-end goods more than pent-up demand can offset. Bain & Company estimates mainland China’s personal-luxury market contracted 18–20% in 2024 and may remain roughly flat in 2025. That slowdown already appears in LVMH’s figures: first-quarter revenue from Asia (excluding Japan) fell 11%, more than twice the group average. Bain expects mid-single-digit growth to resume in 2026 as Beijing’s stimulus and a stabilizing property sector rebuild confidence. Chinese consumers already represent about one-quarter of global luxury spending and could reach 35–40% by 2030, so an extended malaise would dent LVMH’s top line even if scarcity supports margins. The Wall Street Journal likewise warns that simultaneous softness in China and the U.S., together about half of luxury demand, could drag sector revenue down 2% this year.

Reliance on Chinese shoppers is another swing factor: any economic slowdown or travel curbs in China would pose a headwind. Results are also concentrated in two maisons, Louis Vuitton and Dior, which generate a disproportionate share of profit; a misstep at either label would weigh on group numbers. Finally, the stock’s premium valuation could magnify share-price volatility if growth disappoints. Investors should be ready for occasional bumps on an otherwise smooth road.

Conclusion

LVMH’s first quarter of 2025 showed that its scarcity-first strategy keeps the group strong even in a tricky climate. The deliberate supply limits at Louis Vuitton, Dior, and other maisons are more than marketing tricks; they form the backbone of brand equity and margins. This approach has cultivated deep customer loyalty and firm pricing power. This gives LVMH a resilience few companies can match when the economy softens. Sales stayed strong after last year’s surge as a result of fresh product launches, selective distribution, and hands-on oversight at each maison. For investors, the company provides steady cash flow and dependable returns while continually refreshing its brands. Its collection of iconic labels, built up over decades, forms a moat that rivals struggle to cross.

Real luxury feeds on scarcity, and LVMH knows this better than anyone. With that edge intact, the company appears ready to stay ahead of the field for years to come. Assuming management delivers the high-single-digit operating-income growth it has implicitly signaled, I see organic sales dipping a further 2–3% in 2025 and rebounding to roughly +5 % in 2026 as Chinese demand stabilizes and U.S. spending firms. This would pull the forward P/E below 18 and could restore double-digit annual returns. At a market value of about $277 billion, the shares still look reasonably priced.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: If Tariffs Don't Stick, Try Taxes
data.description:
Wall Street limped into the weekend like a diplomat on day five of a failed summit. Sure, inflation is inching toward 2% like the Holy Grail wrapped in red tape, but there’s a strong sense that someone, somewhere, will soon impose a tax.

Level Change 5/30/25 (%)

+0.1 Dow

-0.3 Nasdaq

-0.1 Nasdaq 100

-0.0 S&P 500

-0.4 S&P 400

-0.4 S&P 600

Despite Friday’s fade, May ended on a high note. The S&P and Nasdaq logged their best month since November 2023 - up 6.2% and 9.6%, respectively - fueled by AI exuberance, a few economic reprieves, and a US tariff policy that turned out less fearsome than feared. It helps that “Trump always chickens out” - the TACO trade.

That could change.

Trade once again hogged the spotlight, with President Trump jumping on Truth Social to accuse China of dragging its feet on tariff rollbacks. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent echoed the frustration, calling talks “a bit stalled,” though he dangled the prospect of a Trump-Xi call. China, for its part, declined to confirm or deny the existence of Donald Trump.

USTR Jamieson Greer said Beijing removed tariffs as promised but is dragging its feet on non-tariff countermeasures, like rare earth restrictions and blacklisting US firms. That’s a problem when your green economy needs Chinese magnets to stay, well, magnetic. If only someone had thought this through in advance. Maybe, I don’t know... planned?

And just when you thought this trade war couldn’t get more inventive, along comes Section 899, the latest weapon from our Beltway Bureau of Unintended Consequences. The so-called “capital war clause” in the “One Big Beautiful Bill” would hike US taxes on investors from “discriminatory” countries. Think France, with its 3% digital services tax aimed squarely at US megatech, or Germany, reportedly weighing a 10% version of its own.

That could weaken foreign demand for Treasuries, just when the US needs every bond buyer it can get. As Deutsche Bank warned, “weaponization of US capital markets into law” may sound patriotic, but does risk spooking the very investors we count on to fund the deficit.

Well, at least the Fed got a rare win.

April’s core PCE rose just 0.1% month-over-month, pushing the year-over-year rate to a post-2021 low of 2.5%. It was the second straight cooler print and lent some credence to the idea that disinflation isn’t a myth after all, even if tariffs haven’t shown up in the numbers yet. No, it’s not enough to pry the Fed from its wait-and-see wall stare, but we can leave the Eccles smelling salts in the drawer.

Costco (COST +3.1%) rode consumer anxiety to a gain, touting tariff workarounds and the timeless appeal of bulk peanut butter. Dell (DELL -2.1%) missed on EPS but dazzled with $7B in AI server shipments and an order book stuffed to the gills, proving that the next best thing to printing money is being Nvidia (NVDA)-adjacent.

And with that, May closed on a cautious high: tariffs unresolved, tax threats rising, inflation cooling, and AI still gunning for humanity’s user manual.

June arrives Monday. Brace for another chapter in the saga titled Economic Policy as Performance Art.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Where To Look For Opportunities As America Remakes Its Economy
data.description:
Concerns about on again, off again tariff talk, as well as large transformative tax reforms under consideration in the U.S. Congress, are helping to fuel market uncertainty. Michael Craig, Managing Director and Head of Asset Allocation and Derivatives at TD Asset Management, discusses the risks and potential opportunities as investors navigate this highly complex landscape.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - From on-again, off-again tariff talks to bond market concerns about the Big, Beautiful Bill making its way through the US Congress, there are certainly lots of factors weighing on the markets these days. So how should investors navigate these highly complex landscapes? Michael Craig is Managing Director and Head of Asset Allocation and Derivatives at TD Asset Management, and he's here to walk us through the risks and opportunities and how he thinks. Hello.

Michael Craig - Hi, Kim. How are you doing?

Kim Parlee - I'm doing well. I think I just almost want to start there and just say, the markets have roared back. Equity markets have roared right back up. And often, in let's say even the semis and the tech and we've seen lots of things move back up. Bond markets, I think, are a little less sure perhaps of where we're going. How do you think about where we are right now in the equity and bond markets?

Michael Craig - So we still have a modest overweight in equities. We've started to cover a little bit of our fixed income underweight, as the yields have risen quite high. There was news overnight in Japan about them withdrawing some issuance because the Japanese market has actually been ground -- the bond market's actually been ground zero for the move in yields.

And so bonds feel like this move higher is probably near the end. If not, we'll have a little bit of a rally. But we're still going back and forth between kind of is Trump in a good mood today or is he in a bad mood today? And this constant debate or negotiation, in terms of what trade looks like in the years to come.

I think with the tariff side, we're probably getting to the end of the tariff story. But make no mistake, this isn't the end of what they're trying to do and accomplish and to remake what America looks like. And what I find -- what's really interesting is the reaction amongst other foreign capitals in terms of what they're going to do. I find that quite fascinating. And for us, it is starting to open up opportunities to look for investments in other jurisdictions outside of the US.

Kim Parlee - Can you tell me a bit more about that, what's fascinating?

Michael Craig - Well, we've kind of looked -- the world right now is selling their wares to the US consumer and taking those dollars and reinvesting them into the US financial system, whether it's in the Treasury market, equity market, private markets. I think this is about to change. I think that foreign capitals - Canada, Europe, Asia, et cetera - are going to look for ways to stimulate growth that isn't as reliant on the US.

They're going to be looking to become more self-sufficient. You heard it in the throne speech today. Many things that Carney or King Charles talked about was leading to more growth in Canada.

Kim Parlee - Production, independence, right?

Michael Craig - Production, independence, what have you. And so this is very different than what we've been used to, and is going to lead to, I think, some indigestion in markets and other opportunities. And so we've moved more capital outside of North America. We've moved more capital to Canada, trying to really diversify our equity mix. There's a big valuation gap still persistent. And I think this is going to lead to better growth prospects in the world, ex-US, when we look back in a couple of years.

Kim Parlee - Is this stagflationary? I think that's what people worry about.

Michael Craig - It's not. I think that's -- near term might be the case. But the stagflation is weak growth, high inflation. So weak growth we get from uncertainty, which I think on the trade side is going to abate. And you saw the US Conference Board show much better expectations of the current situation today in today's data.

And on the inflation side, whatever tariff rate comes through, it's a one-time hit. It's like a tax. It's not something that's going to lead to more demand over supply continuously. So I think there's a short-term stagflation hit. I don't think stagflation is going to be in the lexicon of markets in six months from now.

Kim Parlee - Can you talk to us a bit about just yields in terms of what we've seen with the 30-year and the 10-year? And people make much ado about it crossing certain thresholds like 5% and what that means. Does it mean what we think it means? Or does it mean something different?

Michael Craig - Well, it's sometimes when you see higher yields, it's a reflection of better growth.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Michael Craig - That would be a mistake. In the US, there has been some issue with deficits. The US is having to raise an extra $2 trillion a year. And if you take what I just recently said and take it to the next level, if countries now -- if there's more of a global demand for capital, if Canada wants to do infrastructure and Germany wants to do defense, et cetera, then the cost of capital has got to go higher. And that's going to be reflected in the bond market.

So I think the press is really hyping up the US deficit story. And it is a problem, no question. But I think what's much more interesting is that countries are now starting to look at that capital, that excess savings in their own country that's being exported and saying, we'd like some of that. Because we'd like to have our own. We have our own objectives.

And so I don't see a precipitous increase in yields. But I also think that Canadian 10-year rates shouldn't be at 3%. They should probably be more like 3.5% if we were able to raise our growth profile like the Liberals want to do. So I -- with fixed income right now, I mean, yields are actually pretty attractive.

The movement in yields is uncomfortable. But the all-in yield you're earning from coupon is not bad. But we're going to a level where investors are demanding higher and higher rates. And I don't think that we're going anywhere near where we were in the previous decade, back to 2%, 1% yields. I think those days are over. So if you're hoping for this big drop in yields, if you're looking to refinance, I would caution against that.

Kim Parlee - What does it mean for US dollar as reserve currency? This comes up every five seconds.

Michael Craig - There's a tactical answer, the strategic answer to that. Tactically, let's just say the next six weeks.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Michael Craig - The market is very bearish, the dollar. And so today it had a bit of a move. I wouldn't expect to see a bit of a lift in the dollar. But once we get through that strategically, I think all roads lead to a weaker dollar. I just look at this set-up and everyone's talking about it. I don't think anyone's positioned for it.

And some of the things -- as we move away from trade, there's no chatter within the US administration about taxing assets in the US. There's a lot of tax treaties that we don't pay tax on private or public investments in the US. They're now looking at that. And that is the other side of trade.

I mean, there might be a trade imbalance, but it's also a capital imbalance. And if that's the direction they go, dollar is going a lot lower. And I would caution investors, we've had a bit of a free lunch last many years. Last 10 years, about 4% per year of US returns has come from Canadian dollar depreciation.

Kim Parlee - Yes.

Michael Craig - That's a lot. I don't think that tailwind exists anymore.

Kim Parlee - It's a headwind now.

Michael Craig - It's a headwind. And I think with investor portfolios, I wouldn't say, we have lots of US assets, but we're looking to rebalance that away. Because I'm mindful that I think that dollar is going to be a real headwind over the next handful of years.

Kim Parlee - I've only got 30 seconds for this part, but I want to ask about President Trump. But the last time we spoke, you mentioned that you thought he was expending a lot of political capital on the tariffs with the ordinary American. Do you have any thoughts? How do you think about his presidency from an investment standpoint?

Michael Craig - They got to get off the tariff issue quick or they're going to get -- or the midterms is going to be really messy for them. So that's why even -- Europe's the last bastion where he was puffing his chest and saying, we're doing 50. And then they walked that back, and they pushed that deadline forward.

Like I said, I don't think he's got the time to keep this issue going. When you look at voter preference in the US, it's right at the bottom of what the general American voter cares about. And a lot of his base, particularly those who are really sensitive to increasing costs, are going to be hurt by higher costs. So I think it's -- like I said, I think by the end of the summer, they've moved on to something else.

That's why I'm worried that they're going to look at foreign assets because they're not voters. And if you're -- someone's going to pay for this imbalance. And that's where he might turn to.

Kim Parlee - You talked a little bit about being modest overweight on equities. And so I wouldn't mind just talking about each asset and tell us where you think. And then finish up with the Canadian dollar. Tell me a bit more about within equities, what's you're -- where you're at?

Michael Craig - So we still like -- we like financials, particularly on the deregulation. And I think I mentioned earlier, I think the tariff story is later innings. And the deregulation story hasn't even started yet. The US financial system has arguably overregulated itself as a response of '08. And there's a lot of regulation that will likely lead to release of bank capital.

So we like the financials. We like industrials in terms of the focus on build in America. We've got positions in Europe as well as in adding to Canada. Again, valuations are more attractive. You get lower growth. But my argument earlier about these countries looking for investments actually should lead to higher growth profiles.

So still like equities, but are a bit more mindful and probably more of a trading mode than a strategically long and just let it ride mode, if you will. On bonds, we've had a material underweight, probably one of the largest we've had in a long time. We started to take a bit of that back. It has been the right call. Bond yields have moved higher.

But at these levels now, we're starting to add a bit to fixed income from cash. And then continue to build out in the private side. Infrastructure and commercial mortgages are a particular areas of interest. In terms of adding resilience to our portfolios, we absolutely don't believe that we're out of the woods in terms of the high levels of volatility, although April was probably the peak knock on wood volatility.

But I think there's still 3.5 years left in this presidency. So there's still likely periods of market turmoil. And my point, if they do look at putting forward taxes on foreign ownership, that could be quite negative. So not getting too carried away, but trying to really maximize the diversification of our portfolios right now.

Kim Parlee - I'm just kind of mentally trying to balance, net it out with, you say, foreign ownership. But alt assets, private infrastructure. I think all the buildout happening is quite positive. But is there something you have to be careful of with what you just said?

Michael Craig - Yeah, sure. I mean, if you've got US infrastructure, just like anything, there is a risk that you could see taxes. Now, again, the argument is that they're going to target countries where they feel they've got unfair tax policy that's hurting the US. In Canada, our digital services tax has been a thorny issue.

Kim Parlee - Which we saw in this Big, Beautiful Bill.

Michael Craig - Yeah. And so this is where, again, Trump starts with a big position then tries to negotiate from there. So I have no way to think that this is going to become law. But it's a risk on the horizon. It's in his mind. And so it's something to be mindful of, in terms of how it would hit us.

Kim Parlee - You mentioned that you're underweight -- modestly underweight the US dollar. What does that mean for the Canadian dollar? Does that mean that you see it going up?

Michael Craig - So I was on a panel last month and this came up. And the question was, we're looking at it inverse, so is the Canadian dollar going to sell off to 145 or rally to 130? My view is that, forget about that. Is it going back to 120 or 110? 120 would be $0.80 and 110 would be kind of low $0.90s over time.

Afterwards, I spoke with one of the economists from another bank. And he said the other day, I got to where you're thinking. So I'm not alone in this view. I think that as the flow of capital starts to slow into the US market, and if we are able to generate better growth here-- and interprovincial trade is like a real easy one.

Kim Parlee - It's real.

Michael Craig - It's real and it's right away positive to growth. It has actually major impacts to our growth. I mean, IMF has calculated for every 10% drop in inter-provincial trade barriers, it would add about 20 basis points to GDP.

So this could be a huge positive. I put those two things together. And I don't see any reason why the Canadian dollar should be this weak. So I think the CAD is going to be stronger. I think 120 would be my target over the next few years, if we -- execution is everything. So there's always that risk. But if we do execute on it, then that's where I think it's going.

Kim Parlee - I'm thinking about Canadian business people. I mean stronger loonie, better for buying things. But then again, tariff and all that good stuff. And everything kind of balances out.

Michael Craig - Yeah, but I mean, we've gotten really -- as a country, we've gotten pretty sloppy about our trade. We've way over-relied on the US market. 73% of our exports go there. We can only send oil one way. I mean, there's a lot of things where there's a low-hanging fruit for us to better balance our trade portfolio in terms of who we trade with.

This is well-recognized. And as you saw today, it is the focus of the government. I also think it's interesting that Carney has sent a single letter to all his ministers, the same letter of the objectives of the government. They are laser focused on growth and improving productivity in this country. So if anything else, I think they're going to be successful there.

Original Post
